data.title: Pinpointing Skyworks Loss At Apple And More
data.description:
Skyworks Solutions (SWKS) reported stellar earnings with significantly above consensus guidance. The stock price traded modestly higher. But this isn't the important story, in our view. The important story is determining the value behind the latest Apple (AAPL) content loss, the second in two years. Philip Gordon Brace, the new CEO, noted this at the KeyBanc Conference, on the nature of the loss: "The reality is we don't exactly know. That's our best guess based on what it is." The CEO referenced 20%–25%. Continuing with comments reaching into the future,

"So the thing you love and you hate about the industry we're in, it's hypercompetitive. But as soon as it's over, you suit back up and you got a chance to win it again."

Brace made it clear that going forward Solutions wasn't backing down, even with its back to the corner, so to speak. The company plans attacks to regain market share. Still pin-pointing a loss and when it might occur is of paramount importance, offering investors value into timing for entrances. When it appears likely known, this will offer investors a dreaded buying opportunity. This also continues our coverage, the last being, Skyworks Solutions Wisely Addresses Its Issues. So let's get to it, firing darts at the target.

The Quarter

In order to pin down timing, the first task includes reviewing the quarter results and guidance. But before we begin, we must note the different management approach to now discussing the business. It was refreshing. Now, Skyworks reported:

  • $965 million in revenue.
  • Earnings of $1.33.
  • A tax rate of 11%.
  • FCF of $253 million.
  • Raised the dividend two cents to $0.71 (4% yield).
  • One customer equaling 63%.
    • Split of 85% mobile, 15% broadmarkets (Wi-Fi).
    • $517 million in mobile, $91 million in broadmarkets.
  • Mobile at 62% and broadmarket at 38%.
    • Broadmarket businesses continue to grow at low single digit rates year over year.
    • Mobile is expected to grow at "mid-single-digit sequential" (quarter over quarter).
  • Android at $100 million per quarter.
    • "The biggest growth this past quarter has actually been a ramp at a large Mountain View-based customer for which we have 100% RF content."

The report included a note about strong seasonal trends in Android and with Apple.

For guidance, management gave $1.0B to 1.03B. With year-over-year flat revenue, this is a number most interesting. Wasn't a major Apple loss expected; thus, wouldn't the guidance reflect it?

A First Loss Calculation

In order to calculate a first-pass loss, an inclusion of both Qorvo (QRVO) and Cirrus Logic (CRUS) reports is added. A table helping to decipher the Solution guidance follows. We are targeting possible unit growth, hiding the loss within the guidance.

Competitor Results Sept. 25 Sept 24 Growth from ASPs Market Growth
Cirrus Logic $600M * $540M $20M ** $40M/7.5%
Qorvo $1.03B $1.05B $50 *** $50/10%.
Skyworks $1.01B $1.025B

* Cirrus guided $510 - $570.

** Cirrus expects PC growth quarterly to double from the $10s of millions. Also, higher ASPs within its camera business at some value impact growth.

*** A few adjustments are needed between the two years. Qorvo added a 10% growth in ASP with Apple. With Apple being approximately 50% of the September quarter, the increase equals approximately $50 million. But two-thirds of the year-over-year decline in lower Android business at $175 million will occur in the September and December quarters, equaling approximately $60 million. Also, the two other sectors grew at approximately 10% year-over-year, or $40 million. The net difference is a $50 million possible unit growth with Apple.

It appears, with the year-over-year Solution's unchanged revenue while peers are seeing a 10% increase from unit volumes, masked the loss at some level. Apple is approximately 65% of the business. Broadmarkets is expected to be approximately 40%, with growth of approximately 5% year over year. If Skyworks is also seeing a 10% improvement in unit volumes and Apple sells approximately 70% of the iPhones as new phones, the company is experiencing a 15% (10%/0.70) lower ASP thus far. This is strange, in our view. Apple parts delivered in September are heavily weighted toward new launches. Investors might expect a deeper Apple revenue drop in the December quarter with a second drop next year in the September quarter when the balance of the sold phones missing Skyworks Solutions sees a full impact. We expect the December quarter to be more informative.

The Newer Company

The story doesn't end with the analysis. We noted above the rich depth added by the new CEO on the business and company direction. The CEO noted tailwinds in the broadmarket sector: "Wi-Fi tailwinds, auto tailwinds, industrial IoT tailwinds, timing, power products." The sector is now $1.5 billion with growth and higher margins.

Brace explained a very important company change with more upfront engineering creating state-of-the-art. The CEO noted that the company probably has been more than he liked of just-in-time engineering. Quoting "right ahead of the curve rather than the just-in-time approach of the past." This is monumental, huge. This change in itself signals a very positive sign to watch for investors. Also, remember, this kind of change doesn't happen overnight.

Another interesting disclosure included the size of the auto business at $60 million per quarter. The continued need for connected auto technology generates a nice tailwind going forward.

Marketplace

Analysts quizzed Brace constantly in the call about future ASP and other growth. When asked a question about winning future Apple business back, the CEO explained that Apple's direction in suppling its own modem opened doors for future business in which it could gain back ASPs. He also explained that the business was and is extremely competitive, with forward development critical to winning new business.

Unit growth from AI was addressed. It was noted that upgrade cycles are now unseasonably long over four years. AI might or should lower this length. The last big cycle in iPhones was 12. Also, the RF business is expected to be positively impacted by increased demand on the transmit side of mobile.

On the topic of Wi-Fi, Brace made this interest comment,

"I think we are in maybe the second inning of WiFi 7, maybe second inning. And we're not even in spring training for Wi-Fi 8. So I mean, I think WiFi 8 is probably '28, '29, and we got WiFi 7 until then, right?"

Both Wi-Fi 7-8 require significantly higher ASPs and are at this point Skyworks best opportunity to grow year over year.

The Chart

A day chart from TradeStation Securities follows.

Notice the point of resistance at $80.

Risk & Investment

Skyworks muffed a punt or two during the last few years, choosing to create just-in-time products too often, a method not conducive to Apple's long-term planning culture. It lost meaningful sockets. Brace plans to change this and will more than likely succeed. It takes a few years. Finding places for long-term purchases remains the challenge. We believe that another shoe must fall—November guidance, for example—before investors might put a toe in. What shoe? A clearer picture of the value of the loss is one. Another very reliable pattern occurs with companies tied to Apple. The price tends to peak with the announcement of the new products. The price tends to fall for some months afterward. Thus, we rate the stock a hold until otherwise determined, in particular, the future becoming a little clearer. But this investment once again is a must-follow. We should also note that our ratings are price-sensitive. A price in the middle $60s would for sure change our view.

Management is opening the door for a view inside. It is quite nice, to be honest, but not yet.

data.title: Is The Employment Picture Really So Strong?
data.description:
By Ryan McMaken

If we are to take the official unemployment rate as the final word on the employment situation - as Fed Chairman Jerome Powell instructed us to at last month’s FOMC press conference - then we’re likely to conclude that the job market is still red hot.

After all, at 4.2 percent, the July unemployment rate suggests that the US economy is at what the technocrats call “full employment.” But at the same time, there is mounting evidence that people are running out of money to spend - and that would seem to clash with the idea that the economy is at full employment.

For example, the most recent three months of the Case-Shiller home price report has shown the index is in negative territory. Moreover, delinquencies in credit cards, auto loans, and student loans are at the highest levels since the Global Financial Crisis.

In another development this week that should be taken as a red flag about household budgets, McDonald's (MCD) restaurants have announced price cuts. Specifically, CNN reports:

McDonald’s is slashing the prices of its combo meals just a few weeks after the CEO publicly admitted that its menu has gotten too expensive and pledged to fix the problem. ...

McDonald’s will also expand its combo offerings with a $5 breakfast deal and an $8 Big Mac and McNugget special in the coming months with the reintroduction of the “Extra Value Meals” branding.

Now, why would McDonald's need to slash prices if the US economy is in a state of full employment? Why would auto loan delinquencies be at fifteen-year highs with a job market in such excellent shape?

The likely answer is that consumers simply don’t have the funds to cover both large auto-loan payments and expensive takeout meals anymore. Until recently, they did have the money to do it, as suggested by the fact that until late 2022, auto-loan delinquencies were going down.

2022, of course, was when we saw CPI inflation rise to 40-year highs. Even then, consumers didn’t seem to care that McDonald’s prices soared from 2022 and into 2023. It wasn’t until mid 2023, about the time when a social media post about an $18 Big Mac value meal went viral, that consumers may have started to scale back fast-food spending.

The consumers who spend freely without care appear to be getting more and more rare in the year 2025. This should hardly be unexpected given the ongoing downward trend in the employment data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

For starters, we can observe that real wages, adjusted for CPI inflation, are still below where they were in 2021. In early 2021, average hourly earnings were at $29.92. Since then, average earnings adjusted for inflation have gone down to $29.71. In other words, there has been no average real wage growth in more than four years.

Moreover, recent monthly data on job growth has been lackluster at best. Over the past three months, total employment (according to the payroll/establishment survey) has largely flatlined, increasing by only about 35,000 per month from April to July.

(Since April, total payroll employment has increased by only 0.06 percent.) After enormous downward revisions to payroll totals for recent months, the July report showed total payroll employment growth was only 19,000 in May, 14,000 in June, and 73,000 in July. These indicate a substantial slowdown in job growth.

The other measure of employment, the so-called household survey, shows that employment has fallen over the past three months, dropping by 853,000 employed persons since April. In July, the household survey showed a drop of 260,000.

The gap between the household survey and the establishment survey stems in part from the fact that the establishment survey tracks only jobs - both part-time and full-time - while the household survey tracks employed persons. So, these reports could show ongoing growth in total jobs that are largely part time, while the total number of employed persons remains flat.

Given that real wages are flat, this could be a situation of workers taking on additional jobs to make ends meet. This theory is backed up by the fact that part-time job growth has been outpacing full-time job growth since 2021. Full-time work has increased by 7.6 percent since early 2021, while part-time work has increased by nearly 16 percent. In July specifically, full-time work was down by 440,000 jobs from June to July, and part-time work was up by 247,000 over the same period.

This all suggests that the job market isn’t quite as hot as both the central bank and the administration are suggesting. For example, during his most recent press conference, Fed Chairman Jerome Powell insisted that the employment situation was excellent, but said that the Fed is basing this assessment primarily on the unemployment rate.

It makes sense that Powell would say this, of course. The unemployment rate, taken in isolation, presents a far rosier employment situation than does job growth and earnings data. The strong jobs picture presented by the unemployment rate is belied, however, by the fact that the labor force participation rate dropped to a 33-month low in July.

That is, the reason the unemployment rate continues to appear low is the falling number of total workers. So long as workers are leaving the work force, the total number of jobs can fall, and the unemployment rate will still remain stable.

Not coincidentally, the number of potential workers now listed as “not in the labor force” has surged by 1.3 million since April, and is now almost back to the peak experienced during the covid lockdowns. Without this exodus of workers, the unemployment rate would be considerably higher.

At least some policymakers at the Fed have noticed this trend. In the July minutes from the Fed’s Federal Open Market Committee, released this week, employment stagnation was a topic of conversation:

In their discussion of the labor market, participants observed that the unemployment rate remained low and that employment was at or near estimates of maximum employment. Several participants noted that the low and stable unemployment rate reflected a combination of low hiring and low layoffs. Some participants observed that their contacts and business survey respondents had reported being reluctant to hire or fire amid elevated uncertainty. Regarding the outlook for the labor market, some participants mentioned indicators that could suggest a softening in labor demand. These included slower and more concentrated job growth, an increase in cyclically sensitive Black and youth unemployment rates, and lower wage increases of job switchers than job stayers. Some of these participants also noted anecdotes in the Beige Book or in their discussions with contacts that pointed to slower demand. Furthermore, a number of participants noted that softness in aggregate demand and economic activity may translate into weaker labor market conditions, as could a potential inability of some importers to withstand higher tariffs.

This “no hire/no fire” job market now appears to be playing out broadly in the economy. While it’s true we are not seeing major layoff events, we’re also not seeing much in the way of new hires.

Now, as I write this, Jerome Powell is concluding his remarks at the Fed’s Jackson Hole conference, and the consensus is that Powell is now subtly suggesting that weakness in employment will require a reassessment in the Fed’s stance on interest rates going forward.

Translation: Powell is saying that the economy has weakened to the point that the Fed now may want to force down interest rates in hopes of stimulating more job growth.

I don’t know if Powell actually believes this, and one can never be sure how much Fed messaging is based on honest analysis. In any case, Powell’s claim that the weakening job market requires more monetary stimulus would certainly fit with the BLS data that Powell casually waved off a few weeks ago.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Arcutis: ZORYVE's Multi-Indication Momentum And Margin Expansion Justify A Bullish Re-Rate
data.description:
Introduction

My last look at Arcutis Biotherapeutics (ARQT) was in April. The company markets ZORYVE for plaque psoriasis, mild to moderate atopic dermatitis, and seborrheic dermatitis. Back then, it was also awaiting FDA approval for scalp and body psoriasis. While ZORYVE has utility in those conditions, the competition is fierce, and ARQT already appears priced for a lot of success. My “reverse DCF” model revealed that Arcutis’ valuation implied >25% YoY revenue growth and 20% free cash flow margins. Moreover, Arcutis was facing an IP battle on many fronts. The active ingredient of ZORYVE (roflumilast) has been around for nearly 15 years, although Arcutis contests that a multitude of patents should extend its exclusivity for many more years. My primary issue was the premium valuation, which seemed to be “priced to perfection.” I gave ARQT a “Hold” rating. Its stock has since increased 16%, lagging behind the S&P 500’s 28% rally during the same time.

Q2 Earnings Recap

Let’s start by reviewing Arcutis’ Q2 earnings report. Net product revenue soared 164% YoY to $81.5 million (up 28% sequentially). Growth was driven by ZORYVE and is balanced between indications. The cost of sales was just $7.5 million, implying gross margins >90%. The foam did achieve FDA approval in May 2025 for scalp and body psoriasis. Impressively, ZORYVE scripts surpassed 1 million to date. Arcutis’ net loss narrowed to $15.9 million from $52.3 million a year prior. This is thanks to revenue outpacing expense increases (SG&A), which is a sign that ZORYVE is seeing natural adoption without Arcutis’ team having to market it aggressively. The company generated $0.3 million in operating cash flow in Q2. Arcutis ended Q2 with $190.8 million in cash and short-term investments. Long-term debt is ~$107 million. This seems manageable given that the company guides for a sustainable cash flow breakeven in 2026.

Operational Strength: Prescriptions, Market Share, and Cash Flow Inflection

A critical nugget is that, despite fierce competition, ZORYVE is capturing market share from older therapies and now accounts for ~41% of non-steroidal topical prescriptions in psoriasis.

VTAMA was launched just months before ZORYVE in the psoriasis market. VTAMA showed early traction, but has since given up considerable market share to ZORYVE, which generated $27.7 million in psoriasis in Q2. In terms of indications, VTAMA is catching up, having secured approval for atopic dermatitis last December. ZORYVE’s head start in multiple indications is key to growing mindshare among dermatologists. Notably, in June, Arcutis revealed that the American Academy of Dermatology gave ZORYVE a “Strong” recommendation for use in adult atopic dermatitis.

Regarding Incyte’s (INCY) OPZELURA, its very strong efficacy comes with a boxed warning for potential serious side effects associated with systemic JAK class risks. OPZELURA has been commercially successful, with Incyte projecting up to $670 million in 2025 sales. However, OPZELURA is particularly effective for severe cases or controlling acute flares. It seems that ZORYVE is positioned as a safer, long-term maintenance option for chronic use. Legacy topical therapies (e.g., corticosteroids, generic creams) remain relevant, and, although it’s waning, the ongoing inertia of steroid use could limit ZORYVE’s total addressable market.

Industry Tailwinds and Policy Headwinds

There are also a few industry trends that are driving the adoption of ZORYVE and others. Dermatologists are gradually becoming wary of the risks associated with prolonged topical steroid use (e.g., skin thinning, systemic absorption). So, they are seeking safe, steroid-sparing alternatives like ZORYVE. In addition, payers are increasingly willing to cover new topical agents, as evidenced by ZORYVE’s favorable gross-to-net rates. Simultaneously, there are some headwinds to be aware of. Medicare price negotiations could create Medicare Part D coverage challenges for newer drugs. Moreover, the dermatology market is fiercely competitive with large pharma investing billions in R&D each year. Overall, the trends seem to be supportive of ZORYVE.

Applying the "Dizzy" Framework: Moderate Risk, Manageable Tilt

Arcutis’ Q2 earnings report could easily be classified as a Tier-1 “Decisive” signal (definitive, high-quality information). The earnings release was filed via 8-K and accompanied by a conference call. Exact revenues for each ZORYVE indication were provided. These are the kinds of updates that investors can act on. Tier-3 “Noise” signals may include rumors or unconfirmed social media posts.

Looking ahead, ARQT appears only moderately Dizzy.

Arcutis does have a few catalysts in the next quarter or so. The main event on the horizon is the PDUFA decision (October 13) for its supplemental NDA expanding ZORYVE to children aged 2–5 with atopic dermatitis. Although inherently binary (approval or delay), the risk appears moderate given that ZORYVE’s active ingredient is well-known and it is already approved for older ages in the same indication. As for Q3 earnings (expected in early November), the company has already guided for a seasonal dip. So, I don’t expect any major surprises, although it’s possible the market, despite the warning, could react negatively to it. There are no major unblinded clinical readouts expected in the next quarter. As for Control-Deficit, Arcutis does a good job providing comprehensive data and has consistently delivered on promises (multiple approvals on time). Although financing needs are low, Arcutis’ single-asset dependence creates high Stakes. Any negative developments in ZORYVE’s IP/exclusivity could really impact its valuation. This is a major risk in ARQT that investors always need to be cognizant of.

With only a moderate TI score, it would be reasonable to maintain current exposure but avoid any aggressive moves. The Dizzy framework also emphasizes waiting for full information and a brief cooling-off period around major events before taking action. This could be helpful if the knee-jerk reaction following Arcutis’ Q3 earnings is harsh. We want to avoid trading into volatility. So, we await the detailed earnings call transcript/slides when available. I like to call this a 24-48 hour “stability window.” Its purpose is to avoid impulse trading.

“Valuation deadbands” define price ranges where we take no action versus levels that would trigger a buy or sell. I often tie this in with technicals.

For ARQT, it has comfortably traded over its 200-day moving average since last November. Should it fall under this level ($13.82) in the absence of a Tier-1 event, it may signal an overreaction or undue pessimism, warranting an add. On the flip side, should the stock trade >$20, signaling that good news is factored in, we may consider trimming the position. Between these two levels is our neutral “no-action” band that does not warrant any trades.

IP Watch: Patents Strengthen, Generic Pathway Emerges

As for ZORYVE’s IP, Arcutis continues to rack up Orange Book-listed patents in its attempt to “thicken the fence.” The company believes (forward-looking statement) that ZORYVE is protected into 2037. ZORYVE topical products are 505(b)(2) (no NCE), which enjoy only 3-year exclusivity periods (new dosage form/strength/indication). Orange Book-listed patents can provide some protection, but may not be “bulletproof.” ZORYVE cream faces active ANDA pressure, although near-term at-risk launch risk looks low given the still-running 30-month stay plus day-for-day extension and a multi-patent estate. Interestingly, the FDA issued a draft product-specific guidance for generic roflumilast cream in May 2025, signaling a bioequivalence pathway for ANDAs. The draft PSG is a medium-term negative but does not override patents. ZORYVE foam appears to enjoy cleaner exclusivity runways (no visible ANDAs yet). Overall, the IP moat is intact. Generic risk is concentrated in ZORYVE 0.3% cream and is time-capped.

Conclusion

To wrap up, recent developments support a rating upgrade from Hold to Buy on ARQT. My confidence is based on skyrocketing revenue growth, improving margins, multiple product approvals, and strong market uptake. At current prices, I believe its stock offers upside if management executes its plan (its stock should rerate higher as sales climb toward the hundreds of millions and the company begins generating cash). As I alluded to above, there are risks (e.g., competition, generic entry, single-asset dependency) that merit close monitoring. Subsequently, this is a position that requires prudent allocation.

data.title: In Jackson Hole, Chair Powell Hints At Near-Term Rate Cuts And Reiterates Long-Term Approach
data.description:
Friday’s much-anticipated speech by Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell at the annual Jackson Hole symposium did not disappoint markets, nor did the Fed’s rollout of its revised monetary policy framework surprise Fed watchers. The central bank appears on track to lower its policy rate in the near term, albeit with a cautious approach. Over the longer term, the Fed reaffirmed its commitment to its dual mandate while introducing only nuanced clarifications to its monetary policy framework.

Signals about the near-term policy path

Powell first discussed the outlook for inflation and the labor market and the implications for near-term monetary policy. Relative to his remarks immediately following the most recent Fed meeting on 30 July, Powell’s tone was dovish.

He highlighted that, while the labor market appears to be in balance at a level consistent with full employment, “it is a curious kind of balance that results from a marked slowing in both the supply of and demand for workers. This unusual situation suggests that downside risks to employment are rising. And if those risks materialize, they can do so quickly in the form of sharply higher layoffs and rising unemployment.”

By contrast, his discussion of the outlook for inflation seemed to downplay the risk that the price level increase resulting from tariffs would, over time, trigger persistent upward pressure on inflation. In particular, the Fed judged that “given that the labor market is not particularly tight and faces increasing downside risks,” a wage-price spiral (in which employees demand higher wages to meet higher costs of living, in turn pressuring companies to pass along the wage costs) is unlikely to result from higher tariffs.

For the Fed, inflation expectations likely matter as much as the latest inflation data, and Powell’s speech also downplayed the risk tariffs could push inflation expectations higher. He stated that “measures of longer-term inflation expectations, however, as reflected in market- and survey-based measures, appear to remain well anchored and consistent with our longer-run inflation objective of 2 percent.”

Investors seeking signals on the Fed’s rate path likely focused on the passage when Powell said that “with risks to inflation … tilted to the upside, and risks to employment to the downside … [and] with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance.” Indeed, stock markets rose sharply and the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury fell – even before Powell finished speaking.

Powell’s comments did not change our baseline outlook. Instead, we interpret the market reaction as surprise that Powell so clearly opened the door to rate cuts instead of sticking to the maximum data-dependence message from the July meeting.

However, Powell emphasized the need to proceed cautiously, given that risks to inflation are tilted to the upside and a stable unemployment rate hints at a labor market still in balance. We continue to expect 50 basis points of cuts in both 2025 and 2026.

Modest changes to the long-term policy framework

In the second part of Powell’s speech, he summarized the Fed’s changes to its monetary policy framework as part of a scheduled five-year review. The changes are sensible, in our view, and had been telegraphed in previous Fed meeting minutes and press conferences.

We believe the changes will have no bearing on the path for Fed policy in the coming months, and perhaps much longer, because the revised framework simply returns the Fed to the original symmetric 2% inflation-targeting regime it established in 2012.

The revised framework acknowledges that realities in 2025 are much different from the situation the Fed faced when it undertook its previous framework review in 2019–2020. The Fed no longer expects the zero bound on the policy rate to be a frequently binding constraint, nor does it expect the risks to inflation to be skewed to the downside. The new framework statement also clarifies that maximum employment is defined as the highest level of employment that can be achieved on a sustained basis in the context of price stability, and that employment may at times run above real-time estimates of maximum employment without posing risks to price stability. By design, however, the new framework no longer makes specific reference to employment “shortfalls.”

Finally, although under the new framework, the Fed will no longer aim for inflation to average 2% over time, the central bank seems prepared to act forcefully to help ensure that longer-run inflation expectations remain well-anchored at the 2% target. We interpret this as the potential for the Fed to tolerate a moderate inflation overshoot following a period when inflation had been running persistently below target, akin to the Fed’s inflation-targeting policy adopted in 2012.

The changes to the monetary policy framework were sensible and well-telegraphed, emphasizing the Fed’s enduring commitment to its mandate.

Disclosures

Statements concerning financial market trends or portfolio strategies are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. Outlook and strategies are subject to change without notice.

PIMCO as a general matter provides services to qualified institutions, financial intermediaries and institutional investors. Individual investors should contact their own financial professional to determine the most appropriate investment options for their financial situation. This material contains the opinions of the author and such opinions are subject to change without notice. This material has been distributed for informational purposes only and should not be considered as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular security, strategy or investment product. Information contained herein has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but not guaranteed. No part of this material may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without express written permission. PIMCO is a trademark of Allianz Asset Management of America LLC in the United States and throughout the world.

CMR2025-0822-4770343

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: It's AI Freak-Out Time Again
data.description:
Stop us if you’ve heard this one before. The whole investor world is piling into stocks with a hot AI story to tell, driving prices into the stratosphere. Then, out of nowhere, something comes along – a research piece, an offhand comment by someone at the center of the AI universe – that pours cold water over the bullish vibes. Suddenly, everyone is talking up “rotation” and “equal-weighted index” and running as fast as their little legs will take them away from all things AI.

The Rotations That Weren’t

Well, we have arrived at the midsummer-2025 version of this dance, thanks to the combined jab-cross effect of a recent study conducted by a team of researchers at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and some out-loud musings by Sam Altman, the head of OpenAI. We’ll get to the gist of what these deliveries had to say in a moment; first, though, let’s do a quick review of bygone Cassandras signaling the apparent end of the AI narrative.

Just about one year ago, it was a research piece by Goldman Sachs that dared to ask the question: What is all this $1 trillion in AI capital expenditure actually going to do? The point being made was that all that money going into sprawling data centers and voracious large language modules had yet to prove the existence of profitable use cases to justify the outlays. AI stocks pulled back sharply, CNBC pundits announced the dawn of the rotation into value stocks, and then… the rotation fizzled, nobody came up with a more compelling narrative for the market, and everyone went back to buying AI names.

The next head-fake came at the beginning of this year in the form of DeepSeek, the Chinese AI platform that appeared to have nearly the same whiz-bang capacity as their American competitors, but at a fraction of the cost. Once again, investors ran for the hills, pundits wrote the obituaries, and then the rotation faded away. Eventually, the market talked itself into the idea that, actually, DeepSeek was more of a validator for the AI story than a detractor.

MIT and Altman Deliver a One-Two

Now we come to the present moment, on the heels of yet another long run of outperformance by the so-called Magnificent Seven and their assorted hangers-on in the AI space. This week, a group of researchers affiliated with the Massachusetts Institute of Technology published a study with the conclusion that 95 percent of organizations are getting “zero return” from their generative AI investments. In other words, the MIT study seemed to validate what that Goldman Sachs' research article was saying a year ago – that all the jaw-dropping sums being poured into AI were failing to demonstrate how the technology was going to make money for its users.

With the MIT study fresh off the press, investors then went back and looked at some comments made late last week by Sam Altman of OpenAI (the enterprise that gave ChatGPT to the world). Altman remarked that a bubble in AI may well be at hand and that investors had the potential to lose a lot of money before the technology lived up to its full potential (which Altman, naturally, believes is eventually going to be world-changing). Rotate into value, call the pundits, rinse, and repeat.

The Innovation – Adoption Gap

One fact which has almost always been true about new technologies is that there is a window of time – sometimes a very long window – between the original innovation and its widespread adoption. The light bulb, to cite one example, was invented in 1879. Forty years later, only half of all American households were connected to the electric grid. Another example: computers didn’t start landing on the desks of office workers in any meaningful number until the early-mid 1980s. The productivity gains from automating business processes didn’t show up in macroeconomic data until the early 2000s.

It is likely that a similar innovation – adoption gap will exist between the introduction of generative AI, which happened only two and a half years ago, and its ability to demonstrate a profitable impact on enterprise functions. So-called AI agentics – the ability to leverage personnel and related expenses with scalable AI operations – are already out there but have yet to approach any kind of critical mass.

In the meantime, though, we see no signs of any kind of let-up in the flow of investment dollars into AI infrastructure. The companies that are making these investments and own the intellectual property behind them are going to continue to have a compelling story to tell. For that reason, we imagine that the current AI freak-out is going to end the way the previous ones have. Rinse and repeat.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fed Minutes Reveal Governors' Thought Process Behind July Rate Hold
data.description:
Three weeks after the Federal Open Market Committee opted to leave its target range unchanged at 4.25%–4.50%, fresh details from the July meeting shed light on why policymakers are growing uneasy even as they stay on hold. Minutes released Wednesday reveal officials wrestling with sticky price pressures, new tariffs, and a cooling labor market - all while the spot price of gold hovered near $3,349.

Nine voting members, including Chair Jerome Powell, backed the status quo, but Governors Michelle Bowman and Christopher Waller dissented, favoring a quarter-point cut. They argued that, stripping out temporary tariff effects, inflation is already near the 2 percent goal and that a small rate cut would ‘proactively hedge against further weakening in the economy.’

The majority, however, judged risks as tilted toward higher inflation and weaker employment, citing data that show foreign exporters passing almost none of the tariff burden back to U.S. firms and consumers. At the time, the consensus believed services prices were finally cooling, but officials fear new levies could light another inflationary fuse even as growth slows.

Staff projections paint a dour backdrop: real GDP growth is expected to decelerate enough for unemployment to top its “natural rate” later this year and remain elevated through 2027. Yet the same forecast sees headline inflation easing to just 2 percent by that year - an optimism that might impress spreadsheet modelers more than households grappling with higher grocery bills.

Several participants also flagged “elevated asset valuation pressures” and the possibility of unrealized bank losses should long-term yields spike, a scenario that could bring déjà vu to anyone who remembers the last time rates were cut.

The committee nevertheless reaffirmed its plan to let up to $5 billion in Treasury notes and $35 billion in mortgage-backed securities roll off the balance sheet each month. At the same time, it authorized up to $500 billion in overnight repo operations at a 4.5 percent minimum bid, effectively promising a liquidity backstop if markets wobble.

Some members warned that the newly enacted GENIUS Act - aimed at regulating payment stablecoins - may funnel even more demand into Treasuries, potentially complicating monetary control and squeezing traditional banks.

The Fed insists it can thread the needle, tamping down prices without breaking the economy. Given tariff headwinds, balance sheet shrinkage, and a significant equity pullback on Wednesday, markets - and gold buyers - are hedging their bets.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Paccar: A Weakening Economy Will Not Bode Well For This Prospect (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
When I make the decision to rate a company a ‘hold’, my expectation is that shares should see performance that is more or less in line with the broader market for the foreseeable future. Over longer periods of time, there does become an increasing amount of uncertainty regarding how well these calls will play out. And that's simply because company conditions and market conditions change. However, I would say that my history with PACCAR (NASDAQ:PCAR), a business that's engaged in the production and sale of light, medium, and heavy duty commercial trucks, has been pretty good. Since I rated the business a ‘hold’ in October of 2022, shares have seen upside of 85.1%. That is above the 77.3% rise that the S&P 500 saw. But it's not so drastically different considering the amount of time that has passed that I would consider my call to have been wrong.

Fundamentally speaking, things are going well for the company up until last year. At that time, driven by a drop in deliveries because of changing market conditions, revenue, profits, and cash flows, all began to decline. This year is looking particularly nasty as the US seems to be heading to a recession. But that's not all. Even the firm’s operations in international markets seemed to be struggling. Add on top of this how the stock is currently priced, both on an absolute basis and relative to similar enterprises, and I definitely don't think we should upgrade it. In fact, because of my overall concern about the economy more broadly, I actually think it would be best to downgrade the company to a soft ‘sell’ at this time.

A though ride

Lately, things have been getting worse for PACCAR. But before we get into the most recent data available, I think a discussion of how the last couple of years have panned out would be appropriate. After seeing revenue climb from $28.82 billion in 2022 to $35.13 billion in 2023, the company saw a drop in sales to $33.66 billion last year. This decline was attributable to its Truck operations, with sales falling from $26.85 billion to $24.84 billion.

This drop in revenue what is attributable almost entirely to a decline in truck sales volumes. The company went from selling 204,200 trucks in 2023 to selling only 185,300 in 2024. This came as the number of trucks sold in the US and Canada fell from 109,100 to 106,400, while the number in Europe plummeted from 63,200 to 45,400. In all other international markets combined, there was actually an increase from 31,900 to 33,500.

These declines were not driven by a worsening of the business or by poor execution. In fact, market share for the company actually improved in the US and Canada, rising from 29.5% to 30.7%. However, total industry sales dropped during this time, with the number of heavy-duty units falling from 297,000 to 268,100. The medium duty market saw an increase from 105,300 to 110,400. But that wasn't enough to offset the decline in the heavy-duty category. In Europe, the 16 ton truck market dropped from 343,300 units in 2023 to 316,100 last year. And the six to 16 ton category rose slightly from 46,800 to 50,900.

The fact of the matter is that the trucking industry is a pretty good barometer of the broader economy. In the US, for instance, 72% of all freight is moved by truck. That compares to just 12% by rail, 10% by pipeline, and 6% by water. The US alone has 38.9 million commercial trucks, with 4.3 million of them falling under the heavy duty category. These move literally 10s of billions of tons of commodities each and every year. Management has done a fantastic job of growing its market share in recent years as the image below illustrates. But this doesn't mean that it's immune from market conditions.

Sticking to the US since data is more readily available, we need to only look at what is going on with the industry more broadly. One of the best metrics that we can look at would be the Cass Freight Index, which tracks the number of freight shipments across all transportation modes. This includes trucks, but it also includes rail, air, barges, and more. In July of this year, the index hit one of the lowest points it has hit in the last 10 years. In fact, other than December of last year and January of this year, it is at the lowest point it has been since July of 2020, when the US economy was still dealing with the COVID-19 pandemic.

In a previous article, I detailed how I believe the US economy is heading toward a recession. Consumer sentiment is near all-time lows and with tariffs guaranteed to serve as a consumption tax on the American consumer, with a little economic benefit to show for it, the picture will probably get worse before it gets better. When it comes to the trucking industry specifically, earlier this month, one source indicated that spot rates are now consistently below contract rates. The spot versus contract spread is negative, which is a recessionary trend that has not been seen in the US since 2019. That same source pointed out other issues in the space, such as a rise in bankruptcies in this market. In fact, according to that source, equipment repossessions are up 40% year over year.

For now, there have been some bright spots for shareholders. Revenue associated with the Parts segment, for instance, continues to grow. From 2023 to 2024, that revenue increased from $6.41 billion to $6.67 billion. Management attributed this to an $89.8 million increase in aftermarket parts volume, with every market except for the US and Canada experiencing growth. Furthermore, aftermarket parts prices added $161.6 million to the top line, mostly thanks to higher prices in Europe, as well as in the US and Canada. I would venture to say that the fact that volumes in the trucking space are down is a short-term positive for the aftermarket industry since it means that drivers are more likely to invest in maintaining their vehicles as opposed to buying new ones. But eventually, if things get tough, weakness there will occur.

Financial Services revenue increased recently as well, growing from $1.81 billion in 2023 to $2.10 billion last year. This came as new loan and lease volumes jumped year over year from $7.21 billion to just $7.50 billion. But that only has to do with an increase in market share that the company was able to capture. It also benefited from higher operating lease volumes thanks to a growth in demand and a rise in the amount financed per truck in all of its major markets.

With revenue recently dropping, it should come as no surprise that profitability would decline also. After seeing that income shoot up from $3.01 billion in 2022 to $4.60 billion in 2023, there was a drop to $4.16 billion last year. Other profitability metrics for the company contracted also. Operating cash flow, for instance, rose from $4.19 billion to $4.64 billion. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $5.27 billion to $5.06 billion. Finally, EBITDA for the company fell from $6.48 billion to $5.64 billion.

In pretty much every respect, 2025 is looking even worse for the business. For the same reasons that I mentioned, overall revenue fell from $17.52 billion in the first half of last year to $14.95 billion in the first half this year. The company saw a drop in Truck segment revenue from $13.12 billion to $10.47 billion, with management claiming that this was because of a decline in the number of units sold from 96,500 to 79,400. In the US and Canada, the firm saw a lot of pain.

But it also experienced downside in Europe and in all other international markets combined. This shows a deterioration of the overall health of the business. According to the firm, the US and Canada saw a decline in heavy duty retail sales from 127,600 to 120,800. And the medium duty market reported a drop from 52,300 to 49,600. In Europe, the 16 ton truck market reported a plunge from 174,800 units to 151,100 units. And the six to 16 ton category dropped precipitously from 27,700 to 19,900.

Clearly, broader market conditions are challenging and getting increasingly worse. Fortunately, we are still seeing the other parts of the company improve year over year. Parts revenue grew from $3.34 billion to $3.41 billion. And Financial Services revenue managed to rise from $1.02 billion to $1.08 billion. But seeing as how the Truck segment does a lot of heavy lifting in terms of revenue, that decline was disproportionately painful for the firm's bottom line. Its pre-tax income plunged from $1.72 billion to $673.7 million. This was instrumental in pushing overall net profits for the company down from $2.32 billion to $1.23 billion. We saw a decline in operating cash flow from $1.91 billion to $1.74 billion. And on an adjusted basis, the drop was from $2.80 billion to $1.67 billion. Finally, EBITDA for the company declined from $3.01 billion to $1.95 billion.

If we assume that the rest of this year looks like the first half of the year did on a year over year basis, we would expect net income to come in at around $2.21 billion. Adjusted operating cash flow would be about $3.02 billion, while EBITDA would total $3.48 billion. If we use these estimates, and we also use the historical results that the company achieved for last year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. On a forward basis, the company actually looks a tad bit pricey. But in the table below, I compared it to five other similar firms. While only two of those companies were cheaper than it is on a price to operating cash flow basis, the picture looks worse when it comes to the other two approaches. On a price to earnings basis, four of the five companies were cheaper than it is. And on an EV to EBITDA basis, our candidate was the most expensive of the group. Admittedly, these are far from being perfect comparables. But they all deal and significant machinery that is tied to the overall health of the economy.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
PACCAR 23.4 17.1 16.9
Cummins (CMI) 18.5 19.2 12.2
Terex Corp (TEX) 2.1 8.6 10.9
Oshkosh (OSK) 13.8 11.0 8.6
WabTec (WAB) 28.6 19.8 16.4
The Manitowoc Company (MTW) 7.5 23.6 7.4

Takeaway

As things stand, I think investors would be wise to remain cautious when it comes to PACCAR. I believe that, in the long run, the company would do just fine. But with the way shares are priced and my belief that the economy is about to experience a recession, I would argue that downgrading it to a soft ‘sell’ is appropriate at this point in time.

data.title: Is This The Golden Age Of Macro Investing?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

In this episode of the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, Nicholas Bohnsack, President and CEO of Strategas Securities, and Amy Zhang, Executive Vice President and Portfolio Manager at Alger, reveal the powerful currents driving markets in 2025.

Transcript

Mike Larson: Macroanalysis is as important as it's ever been, given key questions about the Fed, the economy, and big picture themes like the AI boom. That's the message I heard from one of my MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast guests this week. From the other, that bottoms up research, the identification of corporate inflection points, and the ability to let your winners run when you get the investing story right is most essential. And that's why I'm so excited about this week's episode.

You're going to get two different world views from two different experts who specialize in two different approaches to the market, and they're both all new guests on the podcast. One is Nicholas Bohnsack, President and CEO at Strategas Securities, and the other is Amy Zhang, Executive Vice President and Portfolio Manager at Alger. Thank you both for joining me.

Amy, Nicholas, or I guess NB is what you said to use for you. It's great that I could have you on the show here. I appreciate you both taking some time out for the podcast. Since you're both first-time guests, I would appreciate if you maybe just introduce yourself a little bit, talk about your background in the business, and what it is that you do at each of your firms. Nicholas, I guess we'll start with you at Strategas, and then we'll roll over to Amy.

Nicholas Bohnsack: Okay, very nice. Well, it's certainly nice to be here and with you both. Yeah. So I'm Nicholas Bohnsack. I'm the President of Strategas Securities or Strategas Asset Management. We are a macro research and asset management house, focused on all the major macro verticals and ultimately our clients. We endeavor to be the one-stop shop for macro. So our clients are institutional investors, wealth advisors, and individuals alike.

Mike Larson: Wonderful. And, Amy, same to you from Alger.

Amy Zhang: Yeah. I'm the Executive Vice President and Portfolio Manager for our small- and mid-cap products at Alger. I run four products - Small-Cap Focus, Small-Cap Growth, Mid-Cap Focus, and ETF (FRTY) which just surpassed $100 million. Alger is -- I consider Alger a growth pioneer, and we've been in business for over 60 years.

Mike Larson: Wonderful. Well, there's a lot to unpack. We'll probably start a little bit at macro and then go from there. So Nicholas, I'm going to go to you first. In this environment, obviously, we're sort of hovering around highs. We've had an incredible rally back from all the tariff- and trade-induced sell-off experience we had in February, March, and April. What is your take on where markets stand? I mean, how would you characterize this environment?

Nicholas Bohnsack: Yeah. It's funny. We go through a pretty, I think, exhaustive only because we do it all the time, exercise trying to poke holes in whatever the conventional thesis is. And truth be told, it's very difficult to find a lot to be overly concerned about with respect to risk assets. And so when you consider sort of that as a starting point, it's tough to fade the bull market in equities right now. It's perhaps tough to fade, even some of the bull market that exists in some tranches of credit. But at the same time, valuations are getting to fairly lofty levels.

Corporate credit spreads are on 25-, 27-, 28-year tights. We clearly have a lot of uncertainty as it relates to policy and the implementation of policy, even if we have some clarification on taxes and tariffs. So the way I look at it increasingly is that the market is pretty priced to perfection. May go a little bit further, but we want to start to train our attentions towards what could go wrong. And there may be a little bit of chop and volatility here as we move towards the year-end.

Mike Larson: Got it. Amy, what about your thoughts on sort of the big picture before we kind of dive into the small- and mid-cap space? I mean, do you tend to align with what NB was saying there, or are you a little more constructive, less so?

Amy Zhang: Well, I'm very constructive on small- and mid-cap growth companies because I agree with Nick that in terms of -- market has been very, very narrow, right? So the big mega-cap -- the Magnificent Seven and the mega-cap has been done so well. And so valuation, I think, is very high. And the valuation gap between large-cap and mid-cap growth companies, I think, is so wide that a truck can drive through it, right?

So historically, usually trading -- you would trade as a premium, given the law of small numbers for small- and mid-cap growth companies. And we still have a lot of high quality small- and mid-cap companies. So to that extent, I think, now I'm very excited. I think finally, we're done with the rate hikes, right? Part of the reason why small -- especially small-cap growth has been the worst performing asset class for the last three years. I've been in business for 30 years, and I think that was the worst three years.

So, finally, I think the headwinds of rate hikes are going to turn into tailwinds of rate cuts. So that itself is going to be a very positive factor in a small- and mid-cap growth. But, of course, it's also fundamentals, with which we focus a lot on. I'm very excited because I think finally, with headwinds turning into tailwinds, we will finally get back to stock pickers market for small- and mid-cap growth. The market is going to continue to broaden.

Mike Larson: I'm glad you brought that up because we had that day or those couple of days after, I think, the CPI numbers where the Russell had a pretty big move to the upside. And that was one of the things some of the strategists said, "Oh, we've been lagging." But we see that part of the market kick-in, and it seems to -- you seem to think this is probably sustainable. Yes?

Amy Zhang: Yes. Because I think labor market probably will continue to be weak because AI is very deflationary, right? Like, so to that extent, that's a transformative force that I think it just and -- economy, hopefully, we can muddle through, but we are worried about maybe the growth.

So growth is going to become scarce. So, what we do is identify exceptional small- and mid-cap companies that have the wherewithal to become exceptional large companies. So when growth become scarce, that kind of company can truly grow through idiosyncratic drivers will really shine. So I think it's going to -- the winner is going to shine through, and that's our job at Alger to be stock pickers.

Mike Larson: Got it. Nicholas, let me pivot back to you and talk about the big picture. I mean, we've got -- we're sort of parsing Powell this week depending what he has to say. We're all wondering CPI versus PPI, what's right, what's leading, and all that. What are your thoughts on that being a market driver one way or the other? I mean, do you think we're overly concerned about the Fed here, under concerned?

Nicholas Bohnsack: Well, we certainly spend a lot of time wringing our hands over what they'll do next. And I'd say political pressure notwithstanding, the Fed is trying to really articulate the last 20 yards of a pretty difficult track, right? You've got inflation, as you mentioned, in some of the PCE and other producer price data that remains pretty sticky. And I think there's a fair argument to be made that some of the tariff-related price adjustments, albeit one time, could lend themselves to sort of re-elevating inflation as a principal point of concern.

So the Fed has a sort of a tricky dance to play. I also think though that the case is building for monetary policy to be more neutral as opposed to restrictive. And that may be what the president is getting at. Of course, the proclivity of most presidents would be for easier monetary policy outside sort of the exogenous circumstances. But I don't know that we're spending too much time on it.

At the end of the day, monetary policy probably has to move back into neutral territory. How quickly they do that is what will provide some dislocation. If you think of the consensus believing that a 25-basis point cut is going to come in September or even as the Treasury secretary interestingly suggested a 50-basis point cut, you look at the two-year, they want 75 basis points of cuts. So there's a fairly wide gap between where the market is, where the prognosticators are, and maybe even where the administration is.

What I look at as we move towards the end of the year is, can the sort of organic drivers of growth - we talk about artificial intelligence, we talk about the industrial power renaissance, we talk about some of the other CapEx incentivized investments that might be made up on the back of the tax bill and the spending bill - can those kick-in before we need accommodative policy to prop the economy up?

Our eyes will really be focused on labor, and I think, the Fed comes back into the picture when you start talking about labor. So I'm not sure that we're spending too much time. It's really a function of how long it's going to take for the Fed to get where it needs to be.

Mike Larson: Got it. Well, let's talk about the earnings outlook here. I mean, you know, we're pretty much through the second quarter earnings season. I think I saw the number. We're up about 11% and heading into season, we're only about expectations were about 4% growth. What are you seeing in the big picture, Nicholas? And I guess, Amy, then I would ask you in the small- and mid-cap space what kind of growth you're seeing.

Nicholas Bohnsack: Well, I'll start with the earnings picture in general. I think two major takeaways from the second quarter. Number one is, of course, we had the post-Liberation Day downward revision, lowering of expectations. You note here in the question that we've exceeded those. Importantly, we've exceeded the levels that existed prior to Liberation Day. So all in a very, very strong quarter in the aggregate. I think the takeaway though is that the mix is changing. So whereas you see strength in tech, strength in financials, you're seeing some weakness in industrials, some weakness in healthcare. So the deck chairs are being rearranged for the second half of the year, and that's why I think those special drivers are going to be really, really important.

The other point that I would make, which has really been key to our, I guess, embrace of the bullish thesis generally is, can companies continue to build the revenue stack? If you can get top-line growth irrespective of how you might manage to the bottom-line, this economy is in really good shape. If that starts to falter, then we may have a little bit of trouble ahead, particularly if inflation remains stickier towards the back end of this year and early next year.

Mike Larson: And, Amy, again, in your space, when you're listening to these conference calls and you're hearing what some of the companies had to say, what's the general story out of the small- and mid-cap space for the earnings season here?

Amy Zhang: Well, we had overall very strong earnings season. And I just want to point out, I think Nicholas is -- when Nick's going to talk about revenue, I think it's very important for us. It's our -- my playbook is really about identifying companies early, right, and the law of small numbers. So we define smallness in terms of revenue when we -- especially for small-cap. So to that extent, it's all about -- for us, it's really all about robust and sustainable, durable revenue growth.

So with that, operating leverage goes both ways, right? So you -- once you have that and then you of course, we focus on company with strong unit economics, then you would trickle down to the bottom-line. So it's really about companies who can grow, have robust top-line growth and also, have margin expansion and a profitability with your EPS growth, and EPS will drive stock price. I think that will be -- because for us, valuation is already so low, right? So it wasn't being compressed. So the companies can truly grow top-line and bottom-line will continue to shine. And those are the kind of companies we focus on.

Mike Larson: Any particular sectors within your space? Or if you're comfortable naming a couple of names, stocks that you think really have a good story to tell here?

Amy Zhang: Yeah. At Alger, we're bottom-up investors, but we're not like looking at companies at vacuum. We really care about the key themes, and we're benchmark agnostic as well. But as a whole, clearly, we've been, for example, overweight industrials. Clearly, with AI, the common sort of theme we are in are data center and infrastructure and so forth. But we also invest in compounders, right, in industrial, which, I can maybe name one, like RBC Bearings (RBC), right?

There's a company that does highly engineering precision bearings. They have mission-critical products like airplanes, submarines, and missile defense systems. So, they have three segments. Aerospace and defense has been fast growing but very strong. Industrial, they have -- their industrial space is very sort of diversified, not cyclical. And, actually, the big and beautiful bill will benefit them. So, to that extent, the company has been generating massive cash flows with strong top-line growth. And, we -- this is a company we invested in November 2020. We still believe that they can continue to be high quality compounder.

So, that's actually an industrial name that maybe not every -- not necessarily a household name. But we really invest in every sector -- it's really about finding the hidden gem. And small- and mid-cap is all about the discovery value. So, one of the -- I know small-cap -- not only small-cap growth has been the worst asset class, but small-cap health has been the worst sector. But even with that, I think we always believe, I always believe, we always invest in companies that we want to save time, life, money, headaches. I think healthcare is still very important.

So, one of the companies that we invested in about a year ago, September last year, is called GeneDx (WGS). GeneDx is a genetic testing company that focus on exome and genome testing for rare disease. So they started with children, pediatrician and pediatrics. And then they -- going forward, we think they can expand. They're already expanding to babies and going forward will be adults. But one out of the ten Americans have rare disease. Half of them are children. So clearly, what they do is very important.

And, specifically, recently, this year June, just a several -- a few months ago, the American Academy of Pediatrics issued updated guidelines that elevated their testing to be first tier test for children with global developmental delay or intellectual disability. So that was a huge validation, even though they're not going to start generating revenue from that specific catalyst until next year. But so far, the company has generated tremendous revenue and profitability growth. We always look for the inflection point.

So when we invest in it, the quarter we invested in September, that quarter, they become profitable. So, for us -- and then they are very small. Their revenue last -- last 12 months revenue is only about $360 million, and their market future TAM could be over $40 billion. So clearly, law of small numbers, and they're really hitting on all cylinders. So, I think still even, again, like, in every sector, there's very interesting players. And, last but not least, they are also very underappreciated AI play because they have a very strong moat.

In genetics testing, data is super important. And especially the interpretation of data is the heart of the whole business that they can do it faster, cheaper, with more accuracy than their competitors, which gives them a very wide moat. And so this is really a very much underappreciated AI play.

And last but not least, we also have a long-term holding in Wingstop (WING). That's a consumer name that you're probably familiar with, highly -- very high-quality company that in a -- I think they're really in the category of one in terms of unit growth and comp, but also highest industry-leading cash on cash return, 70%. But more importantly, I would say, Wingstop is really a technology company that sell chicken wings.

Most recently, they roll out Smart Kitchen, right, with AI engines to predict demands, and they have other -- they use AI in other areas. So, that can cut into, like, say, wait time in half just with the Smart Kitchen. So, anyway, we really focus on scalable, profitable, growth companies.

Mike Larson: Well, my daughter is a fan of Wingstop. So, when she watches this, she'll be happy to hear that recommendation. Nicholas, I guess I would -- same question to you, whether I don't know if you can talk individual names or just sectors. Things that you think look potentially more attractive heading into the rest of the back half of this year, just given sort of your macro view.

Nicholas Bohnsack: Yeah. I mean, for us, macro really comes down to themes. And as you know, we manage a small suite of three exchange-traded funds, and the one that I'm responsible to principally is the Strategas Macro Thematic Opportunities funds, $200 million now SAMT. And what's, I think, important about it is that it's thematic rotation. And so it's not beholden to any one theme at any one time. We can, as the business cycle evolves and the macro-environment evolves, we can rotate among the themes.

And so we have four principally right now that we're invested in. We mentioned artificial intelligence earlier. I do think there's a shift afoot from compute capacity building and large language model development to a more agentic, contextual inference sort of application of AI. That business use case is very, very appealing for some differentiation in the portfolio. We mentioned the industrial power renaissance due to this insatiable need for power, not just by Americans but globally. I mean, the number two use of power in this country is keeping things cold that want to be cold and keeping things hot that want to be hot, and that's not likely to abate. So, the industrial power renaissance is very much in the fore of our focus as we move to the balance of this year and into next.

And then, the two very quickly that we didn't mention earlier, number one is deglobalization. Fortunately or not, I think it's going to define a lot of the investment decision-making that we undertake over the next decade as a group. You saw it on full display yesterday at the White House and Alaska earlier this week.

So, deglobalization is principle, and it cuts through a lot of different threats. If it's intellectual property sharing, defense and rearmament, natural resource utilization, supply chains, et cetera, that's certainly of importance. And then the last one, which I think is a little bit of a -- I'm careful to use the word hedge, but we want to be mindful that things have gotten very rich, is cash flow aristocrats. These are companies that can self-fund in periods of uncertainty, whether that be uncertainty specific to their business or more broadly in the economy. So, from our vantage point, those are the four themes to watch as we move through the balance of this year and into early next.

Mike Larson: I guess sticking with you, Nicholas, I'm just wondering, are there any issues that you think maybe people aren't appreciating enough as a risk factor in this market? I mean, again, we've come -- we've had such a head of steam, and I'm just curious, I don't try and be a Debbie Downer or anything like that, but I'm kind of curious as we head into the back half here if there's something that you think people need to be kind of paying extra attention to.

Nicholas Bohnsack: Well, I'll -- listen, I'll be honest with you, man. When things are really bad, my job and our firm's job is to look for things that are great. And when things are really great, our job is to find the things that may be more worrisome. And so things are pretty good right now. I think the biggest concern I have, as you think about the underpinnings of the economy is what's happening in the labor market. There is a school of thought that says, hey, listen, some of the policies that have been undertaken by the administration or some of the efficiencies that have been perhaps gained by technology are causing some dislocation at the lower income cohorts, and that's absolutely true and valid.

But, on a propensity to spend basis, that's a far smaller group than what we see existing at the higher income and wealth cohorts. So labor generally and consumption looks pretty strong. But if you start to think about some of the tightness in credit, if you start to think about some of the pressures that could be intended on household credit in particular, that could start to weigh on the consumer. And so if I had to say, what's one thing that really worries you, and we're always a tweet away from Armageddon, it seems, it would be on the consumer side in sort of a classical economic sense, as we move through the balance of the year.

Inflation doesn't help that either because it's a regressive tax, obviously. We've managed to keep it at bay because of lower energy prices. But if that should reverse for any particular reason, the consumer comes under more pressure.

Mike Larson: Got it. Amy, and both of you obviously are going to be joining us for the 2025 MoneyShow Traders Expo in Orlando this October. Since, you're a new -- first-time speaker, Amy, any thoughts on, somebody comes to your presentation there, what it is they're going to pick up, what it is they're going to learn? I know it's still a little ways out, though.

Amy Zhang: Well, I hope I'll have opportunity to talk about the new cycle for small- and mid-cap companies that because I think the current policy will favor that. It may take time with lower rates, lower taxes, stimulus, and big beautiful bill, but also deregulation, which will foster more M&A and a small company specifically will benefit from that. And also, just maybe sort of the kind of playbook we have in exceptional growth companies. Because, as you know, the true -- they're only -- it's rare to find those exceptional growth companies. So, our job is to find them early and be directionally correct and let winners run. So, it's really about slugging. And I hope I will have opportunity to talk about those exciting opportunities.

Mike Larson: Wonderful. And, Nicholas, I know -- again, we always say we get these topic titles pretty far in advance. Yours is navigating crosscurrents, policy markets, and themes in the age of Trump. Anything that we haven't covered that, again, somebody's going to be coming to this to see you speak, what they're going to take away from that?

Nicholas Bohnsack: The only thing that I know for sure is that the importance of macro on portfolio construction and trading is not going to abate in the next six weeks. So we are living in, depending on your perspective, the golden age of macro and particularly its importance for investors to consider. So, we're going to bring our latest thinking on it. The title encapsulates sort of the broader lens. We've talked a lot about sort of the pertinent issues today. Those won't abate. We'll probably add a few more onto the stack, but macro is as important as it's ever been, if not more so, and I look forward to chatting about it with all of our guests.

Mike Larson: Wonderful. Well, Nicholas, Amy, thank you both so much for your time, your insights here.

Nicholas Bohnsack: Thank you.

Amy Zhang: Thank you.
